facts_disclosed: Thinking about spending 6 months in Valparaíso to restore a heritage house there

turns.content: do you ever have a building that haunts you? not in a dark way, more like a song you cannot stop humming. I saw something last weekend and I have not been able to think about anything else since
That's a vivid way to describe it. What did you see?

Diego and I drove out to Valparaíso for the day — Santiago in January is merciless, the heat just sits on you. and we were walking through Cerro Alegre, which I have walked a hundred times before, but this time I turned down a side street I had never taken. and there it was. a house, early 1900s, maybe 1910. two stories, balcony wrapping around the second floor with ironwork that looked hand-forged. the plaster falling off in sheets. a fig tree growing through the courtyard wall. and I stood there on that hill with the Pacific behind me and I thought — this house is calling me
That sounds stunning, even in its current state. A fig tree growing through the wall is quite an image. Did you get a sense of the structural condition beyond the surface deterioration?

Diego did what he always does — he looked past the romance and started reading the bones. he said the foundation looks solid, the thick masonry walls are intact, the roof has lost tiles but the timber frame underneath seemed sound. it is the skin and the details that are suffering — the ironwork, the plaster ornamentation, the wooden shutters. everything that gives the house its face. an elderly neighbor told me the family moved to Viña years ago, the house has been empty at least a decade

That's actually encouraging from a restoration perspective. If the structural core is sound, the work becomes about surfaces, craftsmanship, and detail recovery rather than major engineering. A decade of vacancy is significant but not necessarily fatal for a well-built masonry house. Are you thinking about this as a potential project?

this is the part where maybe I sound a little crazy. I keep imagining spending six months there — just living in Valparaíso and bringing this house back to life. I know it is a fantasy, I know I have a whole existence in Santiago, my apartment in Yungay, my firm, the projects we are running. Diego says I am losing my mind. but the heart wants what it wants and this house has gotten under my skin like nothing has in years

It doesn't sound crazy -- many restoration architects find that being on-site full-time produces dramatically better work than commuting to a project. But it would be a big change. You have an established practice in Santiago. Have you thought about what the six months would actually look like logistically?

not really, and that is how I know it is still a daydream and not a plan. the firm could manage without me for stretches — Sofía and Tomás are more than capable. but it would mean stepping back from the Barrio Yungay work that I love, the daily walks past those buildings, the rhythm I have built. Neruda lived in Valparaíso too, you know. he wrote that the city was a vertical madness. maybe that is what draws me — the madness of it. but I do not know. I truly do not know yet

There's a difference between a daydream and a plan, and it sounds like you're aware of which side you're on right now. You don't have to decide anything yet. Sometimes the most useful thing is to gather information without commitment -- find out who owns the house, whether it's officially heritage-listed, what the scope would actually be. That way, if the daydream does become a plan, you'll have a foundation.

gather information without commitment — you sound like Diego when he is being reasonable. I think I will reach out to the Valparaíso heritage office and see what the situation is with the property. no promises to anyone, especially not to myself. but inshallah if the house is still standing when I go back, and if it is still calling — well, we will see. Santiago is my base, my roots are here. but some buildings, they ask you for something and it is hard to say no

That's a good first step. The heritage office will tell you ownership status, any designations or restrictions, and whether there are existing plans for the property. It commits you to nothing but gives you real information. And if the house has survived a decade empty, it's likely to hold a while longer while you figure things out.

inshallah it holds. inshallah the fig tree keeps holding the wall together until I can do it properly. thank you for listening to my daydream without telling me to be practical. sometimes a person just needs space to imagine before the engineering begins
You're welcome. Daydreams are how every good project starts. Take the time you need -- the practical questions will be there when you're ready for them.
